content:
First recall that by the Euclid–Euler theorem every even perfect number has the form
 m = 2^(p−1)·(2^p−1),
where 2^p−1 is a (Mersenne) prime.  In particular m is even, so if n^n+1 were perfect it must be even, whence n must be odd (otherwise n^n is even and n^n+1 would be odd).

Write
 n^n + 1 = 2^(p−1)·(2^p−1)  with 2^p−1 prime.  
Since n is odd, n^n is odd, so n^n+1 is even but
 v₂(n^n+1) = the exponent of 2 dividing n^n+1
is at most 2 (one checks easily that if n≡1 mod4 then v₂=1, and if n≡3 mod4 then v₂=2).  Thus p−1 ≤ 2 ⇒ p ≤ 3.  

The only primes p ≤ 3 are p=2 or p=3.  
• If p=2 then 2^p−1 =3 and m = 2^(2−1)·3 = 6, so n^n+1=6 ⇒ n^n=5, impossible.  
• If p=3 then 2^p−1 =7 and m = 2^(3−1)·7 =28, so n^n+1=28 ⇒ n^n=27 ⇒ n=3.

No other p can occur, and one checks n=3 indeed gives 3^3+1=28, the unique solution.  

Answer: \boxed{3}.

 v₂(n^n+1) = the exponent of 2 dividing n^n+1

The only primes p ≤ 3 are p=2 or p=3.

Answer: \boxed{3}.
